result:
# Static

The coffee went cold again. Third time this week, maybe fourth. She keeps forgetting to drink it because she keeps getting distracted by the window, by the particular way the light falls through the neighbor's oak tree and makes patterns on the kitchen floor that look almost like something but never quite resolve into what they're trying to be.

Her mother used to say that idle hands were the devil's workshop. Her mother was wrong about most things.

---

There is a specific kind of loneliness that has nothing to do with being alone. It lives in the middle of conversations, in the pauses between sentences when you realize the other person is waiting for you to finish rather than actually listening to what you're saying. It sounds like the hum of a refrigerator at two in the morning. It smells like the inside of a car on a winter afternoon, that particular staleness that is somehow also familiar, also almost comforting, the way bad habits are comforting.

Most people mistake this feeling for sadness. They reach for the wrong tool.

---

A man in 1987 kept a journal for eleven years and never wrote anything in it that wasn't a lie. Not maliciously. He simply found that the truth, when written down, became smaller than it was. A Tuesday afternoon could contain everything and still look like nothing on the page. So he invented. He wrote that he had fought a bear near Missoula. He wrote that he'd once held a woman's hand at the exact moment she stopped being afraid of something. He wrote that he understood what his father had meant, finally, by the word *enough*.

When he died his daughter found the journal and believed every word.

Both versions are true.

---

The oak tree outside the window loses three leaves in sequence, like a countdown. She watches them fall and thinks about the word *deciduous* and how it sounds like a decision. The tree deciding. A long slow annual conclusion. The tree saying: *this, I can let go of this.*

She picks up the coffee. Still cold.

She drinks it anyway.

---

Here is something that happens and almost no one talks about: the moment in the middle of being happy when you become aware that you are happy, and the awareness creates a small crack, a hairline fracture, and the happiness doesn't disappear but it changes, becomes slightly observed, slightly performed, even if the audience is only yourself. 

There is probably a German word for this. There is probably a German word for everything that happens in the gap between experience and the noticing of experience. The Germans have been paying attention.

---

Her grandmother swam in the ocean every morning until she was seventy-nine. Even in November. Even when the water was grey and serious and did not seem like something that wanted to be swum in. She said the cold was the point. She said comfort was a slow way of disappearing.

She died at eighty-one, in bed, which seemed like a betrayal of everything she had argued for. Though maybe not. Maybe the ocean had already done its work. Maybe all those years of cold water had been deposited somewhere, a kind of savings, and when the time came she spent it all at once.

---

The patterns on the floor are gone now. The sun moved.

This happens every day and is still, somehow, a small loss.

---

There are people who will tell you that writing is about truth-telling and people who will tell you it is about beauty and people who will tell you it is about making order from chaos, and they are all describing the same animal from different sides, each of them convinced the others have misunderstood something essential. The elephant joke but everyone is also the elephant.

What it actually is, maybe, is this: the attempt to make someone else feel less alone in whatever they are feeling, including the feeling of not knowing what you are feeling. Including the cold coffee. Including the patterns that almost mean something. Including the grandmother in the November water, fierce and temporary and cold all the way down to the part of you that doesn't usually get cold.

---

She rinses the mug.

Outside, the oak tree is deciding again, leaf by leaf, the way all real decisions are made. Not in a single moment but in ten thousand small releases, each one barely perceptible, each one quietly necessary.

She is thinking about calling her mother.

She will, eventually.

She'll let it ring a long time first, like she always does, standing at the window, watching the light do its almost-something on the floor, gathering herself for the ordinary difficult work of being known by someone who loves you imperfectly and means well and is, despite everything, yours.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
